title: Considerations on photochemical genotoxicity: Report of the International Workshop on Genotoxicity Test Procedures Working Group
abstract:
Considerations on Photochemical Genotoxicity: Report of
the International Workshop on Genotoxicity Test
Procedures Working Group
Elmar Gocke,1* Lutz Mu¨ller,2 Peggy J. Guzzie,3
Susanne Brendler-Schwaab,4 Steven Bulera,5 Colin F. Chignell,6
Leigh M. Henderson,7 Abby Jacobs,8 Hemalatha Murli,9
Ronald D. Snyder,10 and Noriho Tanaka11
1F. Hoffmann-La Roche Ltd., Basel, Switzerland
2BfaRM, Berlin, Germany
3Pﬁzer Inc., Groton, Connecticut
4BAYER AG, Wuppertal, Germany
5Parke-Davis Pharmaceutical Research, Ann Arbor, Michigan
6NIEHS/LPC, Research Triangle Park, North Carolina
7Unilever Research, Port Sunlight, United Kingdom
8Food and Drug Administration, Rockville, Maryland
9Covance Laboratories, Inc., Vienna, Virginia
10DuPont Pharmaceuticals, Newark, Delaware
11Hatano Research Institute, Hadano, Kanagawa, Japan
Recent toxicological observations have caused
concern regarding the need to test, for example,
pharmaceuticals and cosmetic products for pho-
tochemical genotoxicity. The objective of this re-
port is to give assistance on how to adapt existing
test methods to investigate the potential of light-
absorbing compounds to induce genotoxic ef-
fects on photoactivation. In general, the Organi-
zation for Economic Co-Operation & Economic
Development (OECD) draft guideline on in vitro
phototoxicity testing served as a basis for consid-
eration. Concomitant exposure of the cells to the
test compound and solar simulated light was con-
sidered appropriate as the initial, basic test con-
dition. Optimization of the exposure scheme,
e.g., a change of the irradiation spectrum, might
be indicated depending on the initial test results.
Selection of test compound concentrations should
be based on results obtained with the dark ver-
sion of the respective test system but might have
to be modiﬁed if phototoxic effects are observed.
Selection of the irradiation dose has to be per-
formed individually for each test system based on
dose-effect studies. The irradiation should induce
per se a small, reproducible toxic or genotoxic
effect. The report includes a speciﬁcation of nec-
essary controls, discusses factors that might have
an impact on the irradiation characteristics, and
gives a rationale for the omission of an external
metabolic activation system. It also addresses the
question that physicochemical and pharmacokinetic
properties might trigger the need to test a chemical for
photochemical genotoxicity. Relevant experimental
observations are presented to back up the recommen-
dations. The working group did not reach a consen-
sus as to whether a single, adequately perfomed in
vitro test for clastogenicity would be sufﬁcient to ex-
clude a photogenotoxic liability or whether a test
battery including a gene mutation assay would be
needed for product safety testing regarding photo-
chemical genotoxicity. Environ. Mol. Mutagen. 35:
173–184, 2000.
© 2000 Wiley-Liss, Inc.
Key words: photochemical genotoxicity; UV radiation; V79 cells; dose-response curve
INTRODUCTION
The evaluation of genotoxic potential has become an
integral part of the assessment of new chemical (pharma-
ceutical) entities for toxic liabilities. A multitude of in vivo
and in vitro mutagenicity test systems have been devised
and regulatory guidelines have been issued. To include the
testing of metabolites into the in vitro genotoxicity tests
with microbial or mammalian cells, the use of metabolizing
systems (usually based on rodent liver microsomal prepa-
rations) was established about 25 years ago and has become
mandatory. The possibility that compounds might become
activated by other means, speciﬁcally after the absorption of
UV or visible light energy, has received comparatively little
attention. No regulatory requirements have been issued with
the exception of aguideline issued by the Scientiﬁc Com-
mittee of Cosmetology, Commission of the European Com-
*Correspondence to: Elmar Gocke, F. Hoffmann-La Roche Ltd., PRNS,
Bldg. 73/215, CH 4070 Basel, Switzerland.
E-mail: elmar.gocke@roche.com
Environmental and Molecular Mutagenesis 35:173–184 (2000)
© 2000 Wiley-Liss, Inc.
 munities (SCC/EEC) on the testing of sunscreens for their
photochemical genotoxicity [Loprieno, 1991].
The potential of a few classes of chemicals such as
psoralens, phenothiazines, or compounds used in photody-
namic therapy (e.g.. porphyrin derivatives) to induce geno-
toxic effects after irradiation has been known for many
years [Averbeck and Moustacchi, 1979; Jose, 1979; Gomer,
1980]. More recently, the photochemical genotoxicity of
ﬂuoroquinolone antibiotics has been reported [Wagai and
Tawara, 1992; Che´telat et al., 1996; Martinez et al., 1997;
Gocke et al., 1998; Bulera et al., 1999; Snyder and Cooper,
1999]. The induction of DNA damage on photoactivation is
considered to be a major factor in the generation of skin
tumors in psoralen 1 UVA (PUVA)-treated patients [Stern
and Laird, 1994; Stern et al., 1997; Mu¨ller et al., 1998].
Fluoroquinolones and 8-methoxypsoralen enhance UVA-
induced skin tumors in hairless mice [Klecak et al., 1997].
The present report attempts to outline a general approach
to the testing of chemicals for photochemical genotoxicity.
In addition to the discussion of experimental procedures, the
International Workshop on Gentotoxicity Test Procedures
(IWGTP) group thought that it would be helpful to give
some consideration to (1) physicochemical and pharmaco-
dynamic properties that would indicate the need for an
evaluation of photochemical genotoxicity and (2) which
battery of tests is to be recommended.
Thus, this report addresses aspects of photogenotoxicity
testing that need to be considered by a laboratory undertak-
ing the respective studies. It does not formulate speciﬁc
guidance for any of the systems thought to be appropriate;
indeed, it was considered that the formulation of precise
guidance was premature at the present state of knowledge in
this ﬁeld.
Because there is often considerable confusion in the use
of terms, deﬁnitions and units, some basic radiological
information is attached in an Appendix.
FOR WHICH COMPOUNDS IS PHOTOCHEMICAL
GENOTOXICOLOGY TESTING INDICATED?
Photoreaction and therefore subsequent photo(geno)toxic
action is not possible without sufﬁcient absorption of light
quanta (ﬁrst law of photochemistry). For human exposure,
this implies that testing for photochemical genotoxicity is of
toxicological relevance only if (1) the test compound ab-
sorbs within the solar spectrum and (2) the test compound is
present in the cells that are exposed to radiation (predomi-
nantly cells of the skin). Testing of photochemical genotox-
icity may therefore be considered reasonable for compounds
intended for systemic use or for direct application on the
skin. The working group recognizes that there is currently
no experimental basis to deﬁne speciﬁc levels for either the
compound concentration in the skin or the molar absorbance
below which testing for photochemical genotoxicity would
not make sense. In this context, it is of interest that the
Organization for Economic Co-Operation & Economic De-
velopment (OECD) draft guideline on the in vitro 3T3
phototoxicity test contains the following statement: “If the
molar extinction coefﬁcient is less than 10 litre mol-1 cm-1
the chemical has no photoreactive potential and does not
need to be tested in the in vitro 3T3 phototoxicity test or any
other biological test” (see Appendix).
When deﬁning classes of compounds for which photo-
chemical genotoxicity testing is of obvious relevance, the
compounds used as sunscreens are premier candidates: they
strongly absorb UV light, and in fulﬁllment of their purpose,
are present on heavily sun-exposed skin. The need to test
these compounds for photochemical genotoxicity was rec-
ognized by the SCC/EEC in notes of guidance revised in
1990 [Loprieno, 1991]. Information on photochemical
genotoxicity is also of value for compounds used in thera-
pies involving light exposure, such as PUVA treatment of
psoriasis or photodynamic therapy of solid cancers. Further-
more, compounds for which reports of phototoxic action (in
vitro or in vivo) have been issued are further obvious
candidates for investigation of their potential for photo-
chemical genotoxicity. Although no extensive correlation
studies have been reported, it can be expected that many
mechanisms leading to phototoxicity (e.g., radical damage,
cross-linking) have the potential to induce mutations if the
DNA target is reached.
The established absence of phototoxic action in relevant
investigations was considered to be a strong indicator that
the respective compound would be unlikely to possess pho-
togenotoxic properties, although the working group did not
reach the conclusion that this fact was a completely sufﬁ-
cient criterion to forgo testing. Photostability of the test
compound was also not considered as a sufﬁcient argument
to omit testing for photochemical genotoxicity as com-
pounds such as TiO2 or methylene blue are known to be
photostable but nevertheless are able to catalyze photogeno-
toxic actions in vitro [Nakagawa et al., 1997; Epe et al.,
1989].
TREATMENT CONDITIONS
Irradiation Schedule
Concomitant exposure of the cells to the test compound
and irradiation is essential to detect the photochemical
genotoxicity of compounds that either generate short-lived
products (e.g., oxygen radicals) or those for which the
molecules in their short-lived excited state react directly
with the DNA (e.g., 8-methoxypsoralen [8-MOP]). Other
compounds that form more stable products upon irradiation
(e.g., polyaromatic hydrocarbons [PAHs]) can be irradiated
in the absence of the cells and the degradation mix can then
be added to the cell suspension. The latter approach would
allow the irradiation of the compound with UV doses that
would be too toxic to the test system. On the other hand, it
174
Gocke et al.

information is attached in an Appendix.

GENOTOXICOLOGY TESTING INDICATED?

action is not possible without sufﬁcient absorption of light
quanta (ﬁrst law of photochemistry). For human exposure,
this implies that testing for photochemical genotoxicity is of
toxicological relevance only if (1) the test compound ab-
sorbs within the solar spectrum and (2) the test compound is
present in the cells that are exposed to radiation (predomi-
nantly cells of the skin). Testing of photochemical genotox-
icity may therefore be considered reasonable for compounds
intended for systemic use or for direct application on the
skin. The working group recognizes that there is currently
no experimental basis to deﬁne speciﬁc levels for either the
compound concentration in the skin or the molar absorbance
below which testing for photochemical genotoxicity would
not make sense. In this context, it is of interest that the
Organization for Economic Co-Operation & Economic De-
velopment (OECD) draft guideline on the in vitro 3T3
phototoxicity test contains the following statement: “If the
molar extinction coefﬁcient is less than 10 litre mol-1 cm-1
the chemical has no photoreactive potential and does not
need to be tested in the in vitro 3T3 phototoxicity test or any
other biological test” (see Appendix).
When deﬁning classes of compounds for which photo-
chemical genotoxicity testing is of obvious relevance, the
compounds used as sunscreens are premier candidates: they
strongly absorb UV light, and in fulﬁllment of their purpose,
are present on heavily sun-exposed skin. The need to test
these compounds for photochemical genotoxicity was rec-
ognized by the SCC/EEC in notes of guidance revised in
1990 [Loprieno, 1991]. Information on photochemical
genotoxicity is also of value for compounds used in thera-
pies involving light exposure, such as PUVA treatment of
psoriasis or photodynamic therapy of solid cancers. Further-
more, compounds for which reports of phototoxic action (in
vitro or in vivo) have been issued are further obvious

chemical genotoxicity. Although no extensive correlation
studies have been reported, it can be expected that many
mechanisms leading to phototoxicity (e.g., radical damage,
cross-linking) have the potential to induce mutations if the
The established absence of phototoxic action in relevant
investigations was considered to be a strong indicator that
the respective compound would be unlikely to possess pho-
togenotoxic properties, although the working group did not
reach the conclusion that this fact was a completely sufﬁ-
cient criterion to forgo testing. Photostability of the test
compound was also not considered as a sufﬁcient argument
to omit testing for photochemical genotoxicity as com-
pounds such as TiO2 or methylene blue are known to be
photostable but nevertheless are able to catalyze photogeno-
toxic actions in vitro [Nakagawa et al., 1997; Epe et al.,
Concomitant exposure of the cells to the test compound
and irradiation is essential to detect the photochemical
genotoxicity of compounds that either generate short-lived
products (e.g., oxygen radicals) or those for which the
molecules in their short-lived excited state react directly
with the DNA (e.g., 8-methoxypsoralen [8-MOP]). Other
compounds that form more stable products upon irradiation
(e.g., polyaromatic hydrocarbons [PAHs]) can be irradiated
in the absence of the cells and the degradation mix can then
be added to the cell suspension. The latter approach would
allow the irradiation of the compound with UV doses that
would be too toxic to the test system. On the other hand, it
can be important to protect the test compound from light
until exposure in presence of the cells to avoid extensive
degradation with the potential to miss short-lived products.
Thus, if prior knowledge about photodegradation prod-
ucts is available a preirradiation scheme might be included
into the experiment. However, the working group consid-
ered that concomitant exposure of the cells to test com-
pound and irradiation would constitute the appropriate gen-
eral approach for screening assays. To facilitate cellular
uptake, it may be necessary to preincubate the cells in the
dark, especially if very short irradiation times are used.
For screening purposes, the irradiation spectrum should
approximate the solar spectrum. Solar simulators, e.g., con-
taining xenon arc lamps plus appropriate ﬁlters (to remove
the UVC and attenuate the UVB part of the emission spec-
trum to the levels of ambient sunlight; see Appendix) are
generally used. The characteristics of the irradiation source
should be described in detail. Speciﬁcally, it is important to
give information on the ratio of [UVB dose rate]:[UVA
dose rate]. Solar irradiation possesses a ratio of around 1:20,
but this value depends very much on location, daytime, and
atmospheric conditions (the longer the light path through
the atmosphere the lower the ratio; clouds and dust further
Factors Affecting the Irradiation Spectrum and Dose
On the path from the lamp to the target cells, the spectral
composition of the irradiation will be affected by the ex-
perimental setup. Special attention should be paid to con-
1. Lids: irradiation through the plastic lid will predomi-
nantly reduce the UVB part of the spectrum (see Notes,
note 1). Household transparent ﬁlm has been described
as a weakly absorbing alternative cover which would
protect against contamination and evaporation [Che´te-
lat et al., 1993a]. It is also possible to use a quartz glass
cover, which does not affect the spectral composition.
2. pH indicators (e.g., phenol red), serum, S9 mix (if used,
see Positive Controls): these components cause absorp-
tion or light scattering that might affect the experiment
3. High cell densities: cells located “in the shadow” of
other cells might be shielded against the irradiation. A
subpopulation of cells might thus receive much lower
doses. For cells irradiated in suspension, this might be
alleviated by sufﬁcient stirring, although the average
dose for each cell will be less than for suspensions with
lower cell density [Che´telat et al., 1993a). Cells grow-
ing at low density in a monolayer should not be af-
4. Test compound: increasing concentrations of the test
substance may reduce the radiation reaching the cells
due to the increasing absorbance. Speciﬁcally, those
spectral regions that are likely to be important for
potential activation of the compound will be attenuated.
Homogeneous milky suspensions or a particulate pre-
cipitate of the test compound will lead to light scatter-
ing and could result in a shielding of the cells against
irradiation, not solely conﬁned to those spectral regions
at which a clear solution of the test compound is show-
ing absorption (see also Irradiation Dose-Response
5. Solution path length: the geometry of the irradiation
vessel will affect the irradiation dose. Cells irradiated in
a narrow, deep vessel will receive less light than cells in
a shallow, wide vessel even if the same volumes of
buffer or medium are used. This is of importance spe-
ciﬁcally for adhesion cultures but also for cells in
suspension. Under extreme circumstances, the UV dose
absorbed within the cells or in their immediate neigh-
borhood (i.e., at the effective locations for short-lived
radicals) might be reduced to negligible levels at higher
concentrations of a strongly absorbing test compound.
6. The environment of the irradiation apparatus: condi-
tions such as reﬂecting walls versus black walls can
inﬂuence the irradiation dose. Because of the geometry
of the dosimeter light reﬂected from the sides or from
the bottom of the irradiation vessel will generally not be
Irradiation Dose-Response Curve
A quantitative description of the various conditions and
their inﬂuence on test results is often rather difﬁcult. It is
recommended that a full irradiation dose-response curve of
the test system in the absence of test compound is included
in the report (see Notes, note 1). This also serves to justify
the choice of irradiation dose, and such a curve might be
more useful for comparing irradiation levels used by differ-
ent laboratories than the physical measurement with a UV
dosimeter or a luxmeter (see Irradiation Dose Measure-
ments). The dose-response curve need not be performed in
parallel to the study with the test compound but should be
run at the same exposure conditions as the actual study.
Intended Reduction of the UVB Fraction of the
The direct genotoxic effects of UVB can lead to high
mutation frequencies or chromosomal damage at irradiation
doses comparable to only seconds or minutes of solar irra-
diation at environmental levels. As an example: 15 sec of
midday sun at a mid-European location (Basel, Switzerland)
induces a doubling of the spontaneous number of mutant
colonies in TA100 [Che´telat et al., 1993a). Figure 1 shows
Considerations on Photochemical Genotoxicity
a very steep dose response of V79 cells regarding chromo-
somal damage and cytotoxicity after irradiation with com-
paratively low doses of unﬁltered Suntest CPS1 spectrum.
Such a high responsiveness to UVB will limit the applicable
irradiation doses to levels that might theoretically be too
low for activation of a comparatively weak photochemical
genotoxin. Furthermore, using irradiation containing the full
equivalent of UVB can mask the photomutagenic activity of
compounds that are activated by wavelengths in the UVA or
visible regions of the spectrum (see Notes, note 4). The
working group was divided regarding whether the UVB part
of the irradiation should be reduced by appropriate ﬁltration
if the test system was very responsive to UVB-induced
damage. Such ﬁltration might most simply be effected by
not removing the plastic lid or using window glass as cover.
It was, however, pointed out that this procedure made no
sense if the test compound exclusively absorbs in the UVB
In continuation of the discussion of this aspect, it was
noted that the working group was not aware of a clear-cut
precedent for an exclusively UVB-absorbing compound
which is established as a photochemical genotoxin. Indeed,
for a compound exclusively absorbing in the UVB region,
the protective (shielding) effect is most likely to predomi-
nate over any potential photochemical genotoxic action.
Similar considerations hold true also for phototoxicity and
photosensitisation. The OECD draft guideline on in vitro
photoxicity states: “UVA and visible regions are usually
associated with photosensitisation, whereas UVB is of less
importance and directly highly cytotoxic, increasing its cy-
totoxicity through 1000 fold from 313 to 280 nm.”
UV doses (more exactly UV radiant exposures) are gen-
erally deﬁned as the energy of the photons with wavelengths
in the UV region divided by the area on which they impact
(e.g., given in units of J m-2; see Appendix). For reasons
described above, the doses of UVB and UVA should be
determined and reported separately. If possible, the dose
Micronucleus assay in V79 cells irradiated with UV and visible
light using the Suntest CPS1 machine. The UVB/UVA relationship was
varied using UVC ﬁlter (1:13), UVC ﬁlter plus plastic lid (1:40), or glass
ﬁlter plus plastic lid (1:100) (data are taken from Kersten et al., 2000).
determinations should be done in parallel to each experi-
ment. This might be done most simply by using separate
detectors for UVA and UVB or by determination of the
spectral output of the irradiation source with the aid of a
spectroradiometer (this more laborious measurement would
likely be taken only at suitable intervals) and then measur-
ing total UV dose in parallel to the experiment. It was noted,
however, that measurements with UVA, and even more so
with UVB, detectors are notoriously unreliable (see Notes,
note 5). This is presumably due to slight variations in the
ﬁltration properties of the ﬁlters around 315 to 320 nm, used
to discriminate the spectral regions of UVA and UVB,
where there is a strong increase of irradiation energy of the
solar simulators and slight differences could have a large
impact. Wherever possible, the dose determination should
be performed under the conditions identical to the exposure
of the test systems (e.g., behind the plastic lid, if used).
If the test compound absorbs visible light, the determi-
nation solely of UV doses is only of partial importance. If a
spectroradiometer is not available, a determination of light
intensity (in lux) is appropriate. This can be most simply
measured with a luxmeter (lux is a photometric instead of a
radiometric unit; see Appendix).
The working group concluded that there are at present
insufﬁcient data to set lower and upper limits of the expo-
sure rate (see Notes, note 6). For obvious reasons the
irradiation time has to be within certain limits of practica-
bility and has to take account of speciﬁc test system char-
1. If not all test samples can be irradiated simultaneously,
one should take precautions that no appreciable
changes in growth characteristics and different dark
exposure periods to the test compound would occur for
subsets of samples during the experimental procedure.
This aspect necessarily favors the use of short irradia-
2. Heating of the cell suspension might cause problems at
high dose rates. This would favor longer irradiation
times. Heating can be minimized by using a fan, placing
the samples in a cold water bath, or by ﬁltration of the
3. Irradiation times should be long enough to allow exact
4. Cells should be irradiated uniformly. If suspension cul-
tures are used irradiation times should be long enough
to allow adequate mixing during the irradiation period.
Uniformity of Irradiation Over the Area of the Test
Care should be taken that the exposure rate at different
locations of the irradiation setup does not vary to an appre-
ciable extent. For test systems that require little space (e.g.,
one multiwell plate), this will usually not pose a problem. In
other cases, such as the bacterial mutagenicity assay where
many Petri dishes are to be irradiated, the plates might be
placed in a circle to have a similar level of irradiance as
predetermined with the aid of the UV dosimeter.
Selection of Irradiation Doses
Three possible approaches were discussed:
1. Keep irradiation dose constant, vary compound concen-
2. Vary irradiation dose, keep compound concentration
3. Vary irradiation dose, vary compound concentration.
While it was recognized that the third approach would
obviously be most informative and that the second approach
has been used successfully (e.g., for screening of ﬂuoro-
quinolones in the in vitro micronucleus test (MNT) test;
[Snyder, unpublished observations]), the ﬁrst approach was
the most often used and was favored by the working group
for screening purposes. The ﬁrst approach is also recom-
mended in the OECD draft for in vitro phototoxicity.
It was discussed that a speciﬁc, generally applicable
irradiation dose (e.g., equivalent to a certain environmen-
tally relevant solar irradiation period) could not be deﬁned
for the various test systems in use because of their different
sensitivities to the UVB effects. For instance, the Comet
assay (Fig. 2) and the various systems using yeast D7 as
target organism [Che´telat et al., 1993a] seem to be quite
resistant to the genotoxic effects of UVB, whereas micro-
nucleus formation in V79 cells is already enhanced at low
UVB doses (Fig. 1). Therefore, selection of the irradiation
dose should be done individually for each test system on the
basis of the biological characteristics of the system. Obvi-
ously, an irradiation dose which elicits per se a strong
photogenotoxic effect should not be used because a further
increase due to the photochemical genotoxicity might be
difﬁcult to detect or might be masked entirely (see Notes,
note 4). One should also be aware of declining genotoxic
effects at high concentrations due to strong phototoxicity
Thus, it would be inappropriate to use a UV dose where
mutation frequencies are already declining due to toxicity
because an artifactual increase of the mutation rates might
be brought about by enhanced absorption of UV in presence
The working group concluded that the irradiation per se
should yield a small, reproducible genotoxic or toxic effect.
When testing sunscreens, it might be of interest to describe
the protective effect of the test compound. In such a case, it
is advisable to use an irradiation dose that elicits a more
Considerations on Photochemical Genotoxicity
sizable effect, so that attenuation of the UV genotoxicity is
Adjusting Irradiation to Compensate for Absorption
As discussed previously, the presence of increasing
amounts of the test compound in the test solution is likely to
decrease the amount of light reaching the immediate neigh-
borhood of the cells. If the test compound absorbs UVB,
this will be apparent from the decrease of the direct UV-
mediated photomutagenic effect (see Selection of Irradia-
tion Doses section). If the test compound absorbs at other
wavelengths, this will not immediately be apparent from the
test results. Compensation of this reduction of effective
radiation doses by either simply increasing the irradiation
time in reverse proportionality to transmission or by using
an appropriate experimental setup has been considered (e.g.,
Che´telat et al., 1993b). Such an approach was, however, not
advocated by the working group for screening experiments.
Selection of Compound Concentrations
The number of compound concentrations should be se-
lected in accordance with the guidelines issued for the
respective dark version of the test system. The criteria for
selection of the maximal concentration should conform to
the guidelines for the dark version with respect to precipi-
tation or toxicity but there are speciﬁc reasons to avoid
strongly precipitating concentration levels (see remarks in
the Factors Affected the Irradiation Spectrum and Dose
regarding absorption and light scattering). In principle, the
maximal compound concentration could be selected on the
basis of appropriate results from previously performed dark
assays. It is to be expected, however, that toxicities due to
compound and UV exposures would act additively, and for
phototoxins synergistically, so that the maximal test com-
pound concentration of the irradiation experiment might
have to be considerably reduced in order to obtain analyz-
able samples. It was, therefore, considered advisable to
perform a preliminary toxicity test under irradiation condi-
The working group suggested to run the experiments with
one, predetermined UV dose. It was, however, noted that for
phototoxic compounds there is limited experimental evi-
dence that decreasing the UV dose, and thereby gaining the
possibility to increase the maximal compound concentra-
tion, facilitates the detection of a photochemical genotoxic
effect (see Notes, note 7; Fig. 3). Thus, if strong phototox-
icity is observed, a reduction of the UV dose levels might be
considered for the repeat assay.
It was considered essential to include unirradiated and
irradiated vehicle controls into each experiment. Unirradi-
ated and irradiated controls without vehicle were recom-
mended to be included if a new vehicle was introduced in
the experiment for which no historical experience was avail-
able. A further dark control with the test compound at the
maximum concentration (as used in the presence of irradi-
ation) should be included, unless appropriate data from
previous experiments clearly shows that the compound does
not induce genotoxicity in the dark. If the test compound is
established or suspected to be genotoxic in the dark it was
considered advisable to test the substance in the dark at all
concentrations used with irradiation in order to differentiate
a potentially additional photochemical genotoxic effect
from the genotoxic effect in the dark.
V79 cells were irradiated with a Heraeus Suntest CPS (xenon
burner) at different UV doses (relation of UVA to UVB approximately
20:1). Cells were prepared for the Comet Assay directly (0 h) after
irradiation or 20 h after irradiation in order also to detect late UVB effects;
2 3 106 cells were irradiated per culture as suspension in 6-well plates for
the 0-h time point and as monolayer in Petri dishes for the 20-h time point.
DNA damage effects were accompanied by strong phototoxic effects
starting at much lower UV doses. Phototoxicity became evident as reduc-
tion of cell number and not as decrease of cell viability (data provided by
For each experiment, the inclusion of a positive control
(i.e., a compound positive only after irradiation) was rec-
ommended. Established photogenotoxins such as 8-me-
thoxypsoralen, chlorpromazine, and ﬂuoroquinolone antibi-
otics are acceptable. Other compounds may be more
appropriate if (photo)chemical characteristics, including ab-
sorption spectrum, are known to parallel the characteristics
of the test compound. If more than one UV dose is to be
used in a single experiment, the positive control would be
tested with all UV doses. The use of positive controls solely
for the lower irradiation dose was thought to be sufﬁcient by
some, but not all, members of the working group.
The inclusion of an additional dark positive control was
not thought to be necessary. In deviation from this general
recommendation, it was considered acceptable for the Ames
test to show a photogenotoxic response for only one strain
with one photogenotoxin, besides using standard dark pos-
itive controls for the other test strains.
Exogenous Metabolic Activation System
The use of an external metabolic activation system (S9
mix) has been a common practice for all in vitro geno-
toxicity test systems, which have limited metabolic ca-
pacity. In the area of photochemical genotoxicity testing,
the inclusion of S9 mix has not been reported to any
appreciable extent in the literature. The working group is
not aware of a compound that showed a clear photogeno-
toxic activity only when irradiated in presence of S9 or of
a compound that would act as a phototoxin only after
metabolic activation (see Notes, note 8). Furthermore, the
addition of material with a high protein content such as
S9 (or fetal calf serum) to the irradiation medium can
produce erroneous results, i.e., can lead to a reduction of
the phototoxic of photogenotoxic effect of a chemical
The SSC guideline [Loprieno, 1991] on photogenotoxic-
ity testing does not recommend the use of an external
metabolic activation system because, “present scientiﬁc
knowledge does not allow the deﬁnition of standard condi-
tions for testing the effect of light on a chemical in the
presence of a metabolic activation system.” The OECD
draft guideline for in vitro phototoxicity test states that, “in
contrast to other areas of toxicology, hepatic metabolic
activation does not seem to play a role for the in vitro
detection of phototoxins.” Inclusion of skin metabolism,
mainly occurring in keratinocytes, was suggested to be
unnecessary on the basis of studies with all well-known
The working group therefore concluded that until evi-
dence for a relevant contribution of metabolic activation is
provided, tests for photochemical genotoxicity need only be
Well-established test systems that have been reported to
detect photochemical genotoxicity are listed below. Appro-
priate aspects of the speciﬁc assays, which have not been
1. Bacterial mutation assays [Jose, 1979; Epe et al., 1989;
Dean et al., 1991.; Che´telat et al., 1993a; Henderson et
al., 1994; Utesch and Splittgerber, 1996]. Of speciﬁc
interest is the deﬁnition of the strains useful for pho-
toassays. Excision proﬁcient strains (S. typhimurium
TA102; E. coli WP2) have been used in experiments
with 8-MOP. They have the advantage that compara-
tively higher UV doses can be used than for the exci-
sion negative strains. TA1537 and TA98 detect the
photogenotoxicity of chlorpromazine; TA98 detects the
mutagenicity of photoproducts of PAHs; TA100 detects
Testing of 2-chlorophenothiazine in the photomicronucleus assay
using V79 cells. UVA/UVB dose in mJ/cm2 (data are taken from Kersten
Considerations on Photochemical Genotoxicity
methylene blue; TA104 and TA100 show some re-
sponse to ﬂuoroquinolones. Based on these data a rea-
sonable battery of strains could conform to the standard
battery (consisting of, for example: TA1535, TA1537,
TA98, TA100, TA102, see ICH S2A guideline, at
http://www.eudra.org) with possible omission of strain
TA1535. Irradiation of the compound and cells has
been performed either directly on the Petri dishes after
plating with soft agar or in buffer prior to plating.
2. Tests for clastogenicity [Dean et al., 1991; Che´telat et
al., 1993b; Yousseﬁet al., 1994; Nakagawa et al., 1997;
Snyder and Cooper, 1999; Kersten et al., 2000]. Chro-
mosomal aberration tests with various cell lines and in
vitro MNT have been successfully used. Most work has
been with adhesion cultures, but suspension cultures
3. Mammalian cell systems for detecting gene mutations.
Tests using the HGPRT assay (V79 cells) and the GPT
assay (AS52 cells) have been used [R.Y. Brendler-
Schwaab, personal communication; Pﬂaum et al.,
1998]. The mouse lymphoma/thymidine kinase assay,
responsive to gene mutations and chromosomal dam-
age, has also been successfully used [Nakagawa et al.,
4. Indicator assays. Photochemical genotoxicity studies
measuring the induction of the SOS response in bacte-
rial systems, the induction of convertants in S. cerevi-
siae D7, and the induction of strand breaks in the
Comet assay have been used [Averbeck and Moustac-
chi, 1979; Che´telat et al., 1993a; Bulera et al., 1999;
5. In vivo assays. Test systems in which genotoxic action
can be measured in skin cells appear well suited to
investigate photochemical genotoxicity under in vivo
conditions with either systemic or topical application of
the test substance. However, only limited data are avail-
able. The in vivo Comet assay was shown to be respon-
sive to a ﬂuoroquinolone [Bulera et al., 1999]. For the
transgenic mutation assays, no complete study has
reached the attention of the working group.
The SCC guideline [Loprieno, 1991] stipulates that both
a bacterial test for gene mutation and an in vitro test for
chromosomal aberrations in mammalian cells should be
performed in the presence of UV radiation. The IWGTP
working group did not reach a consensus on which test
system(s) would constitute a minimal test battery for a
sufﬁcient assessment of a test compound for photochemical
genotoxicity. Because no photochemical genotoxin is
known that is exclusively positive for gene mutations, and
the known photochemical reaction mechanisms are strongly
clastogenic, several working group members felt that a test
for photochemical clastogenicity would sufﬁce for screen-
ing purposes. Because knowledge is limited in the area of
testing for photochemical genotoxicity, other group mem-
bers felt that it would be prudent to carry out a photogene
mutation test if the initial assessment for photoclastogenic-
Note 1. The mutagenic effect of solar simulated light
(Suntest CPS) in TA100 is strongly dependent on irradiation
with or without the plastic lid. An apparent dose reduction
by a factor of 5 was observed (Fig. 4). UV doses were
measured with a Dr. Groebel RM-2 UV meter: UVB was
reduced to 60%, UVA was reduced to 90% in presence of
the lid. The transmission as measured with a Uvikon spec-
trophotometer was: 10% at 290 nm; 65% at 320 nm; 90% at
400 nm. The UVA dose rate without the lid was about 1.2
mW cm22. The inﬂuence of the UVB component on the
frequeny of micronucleated cells and the proliferation index
(PI) for irradiation with light possessing different UVA/
UVB relationships in V79 cells is shown in Figure 1 (for
detailed information, see Kersten et al., 2000).
Note 2. The effect of fetal calf serum as a component in
the irradiation medium was investigated in the photomicro-
nucleus test using V79 cells and lomeﬂoxacin (Fig. 5). An
increasing percentage of fetal calf serum in phosphate-
buffered saline reduced the frenquencies of micronuclei
induced by UV and UV plus lomeﬂoxacin.
Note 3. The effects of S9 mix on photochemical geno-
toxicity of test articles have not been extensively examined.
S9 itself, but also the cofactors NADP and glucose-6-phos-
phate absorb or scatter light in the UV region. S9 mix was
found to completely abolish the photogenotoxicity of the
quinolone oﬂoxacin in the in vitro micronucleus system
(Fig. 6). It was apparent that S9 itself did not cause this
effect but that the cofactors were responsible.
Note 4. The effect of the photogenotoxin Ro 19–8022
was investigated in TA102. Cells were irradiated on agar
plates with light from a solar simulator (Suntest CPS), with
and without a window glass plate that absorbs strongly
below 350 nm. Without the glass, no increase of the mutant
numbers due to Ro 19–8022 was seen (Fig. 7).
Note 5. Members of a European Cosmetic Toiletry and
Perfumery Association (COLIPA) collaborative study com-
pared readings of their UV measuring devices (Dr Groebel
RM-2; Osram Centra; Oriel). The same light source was
measured (Suntest CPS1). The readings for total UV dose
rates (UVA plus UVB dose rates) were found to vary
between 1.5 and 2.7 mW cm22, while the UVB:UVA ratio
varied between 1:9 and 1:118. The variability was mainly
due to different readings of the UVB rates. (COLIPA pho-
tomutagenicity task force, unpublished data).
Note 6. Results with 8-MOP show (Fig. 8) that the
photochemical genotoxicity in the in vitro MNT test was
quite comparable at all UVA dose rates employed (ranging

Note 7. Decreasing the UV dose and increasing the test
compound concentrations made the photochemical genotox-

Note 8. Based on suggestions that metabolites of dacar-

icity, experiments with and without S9 mix were undertaken
using the Comet assay, the in vitro MNT assay for dacar-
bazin and with the chromosomal aberration assay for pi-
roxicam. Dacarbazin proved to be phototoxic and photog-

communication) with and without S9. Piroxicam did not
give a clear indication of photochemical genotoxicity under
either condition (A.A. Che´telat, personal communication).
Diphenylcyclopropenone was reported to be marginally
photomutagenic in TA100 in presence of S9 [Wilkerson et

spond to 10 min of sunlight at noon at equator, or 40 min at
For visible light, the same terms and units can be used,
but often photometric units (instead of radiometric units) are
used. Photometry is based on visible light measurements
that simulate the human eye’s sensitivity for the different
wavelengths of the spectrum. Light intensity, measured in
lux (lx), would roughly correspond to dose rate.
For ionizing irradiation (e.g., x-rays), doses are deﬁned as
energy absorbed in the mass of the irradiated body. The
doses are given generally in gray (Gy) (1 Gy 5 1 J/kg).
The molar extinction (absorption) coefﬁcient is deﬁned as
the optical density of a 1 M solution in a 1-cm cuvette. It is
usually measured at the wavelength of the absorption max-
imum. If the absorption maximum is at wavelength shorter
than the solar spectrum (as is often the case for pharmaceu-
ticals with comparatively small chromophores) one would
need to apply the assessment of the exclusion limit to
optical density values above 290 nm (see also Intended
Reduction of the UVB Fraction of the Irradiation section).
The solar spectrum is divided according to wavelength
[l, given in brackets] in Table I.9
Sometimes, 290 and 315 nm are used as division lines
(this has strong impact especially on the UVB dose values
because of the steep increase of solar irradiance).
Global irradiation dose rates (midday, equator, sea level,
clear sky) are in the order of (Table II):10

day, noon) and of various artiﬁcial sources of light (mea-
sured with Dr Gro¨bel UV meter and Minolta Luxmeter;
meters held in direction of sun) (Table III).11
